# Method Selection Matrix

Converted from `15_method_selection_matrix.csv` so the pack remains Markdown-only.

| id | method | category | buyer_state | use_when | avoid_when | why_it_works | mindset | message_pattern | proof_assets | primary_metric | guardrail_metric | first_experiment | common_failure | ethics_risk | source_spine |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| M01 | High-intent search ads | Demand capture | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer | People already search for the problem, category, competitor, integration, or urgent workaround. | The category is unknown, search volume is tiny, the offer is vague, or the landing page cannot prove the claim quickly. | Search captures declared intent. The buyer is already pulling information; the ad simply routes them to a possible answer. | Router, not persuader. You are intercepting existing demand and sending it to a proof path. | Mirror the exact job or search phrase, then promise a concrete inspection path: demo, comparison, calculator, trial, docs. | Dedicated landing page, demo, comparison table, pricing, reviews, screenshots, integration docs. | Qualified conversion rate by query theme; cost per activated trial/demo; incremental lift if testable. | Branded-search cannibalization, low-quality signups, wasted spend on informational queries. | Build one landing page for 5-10 exact-match high-intent terms and compare activated trial rate against organic/self-serve baseline. | Optimizing for CTR instead of buyer quality; sending all terms to the homepage. | Bidding on competitor names with misleading copy; hiding price; implying affiliation. | Goldfarb & Tucker; Lewis/Rao/Reiley; Fogg; Persuasion Knowledge Model |
| M02 | SEO problem-content | Demand capture and education | Problem-aware, solution-aware | Buyers research symptoms, workflows, templates, errors, comparisons, or implementation questions before buying. | You cannot produce genuinely useful content, the product does not solve the searched problem, or leadership expects instant sales. | Problem-content meets buyers during active learning. It earns trust before the commercial claim appears. | Diagnostician. Teach the buyer to understand the problem better, then show where your product fits. | Name the problem, explain causes, show decision criteria, then offer the product as one path when appropriate. | Long-form guide, templates, examples, glossary, comparison pages, internal links to demo/pricing/docs. | Qualified organic conversions, content-assisted pipeline, activation rate from content visitors. | Traffic with no product fit, shallow AI content, ranking without conversion. | Write 3 bottom/middle-funnel articles around pains that already appear in sales calls or support tickets. | Publishing generic articles that attract learners but not buyers. | Using educational framing to smuggle exaggerated product claims. | Jobs to Be Done; TAM; McKinsey Consumer Decision Journey; Google/online advertising research |
| M03 | Category education / demand creation | Demand creation | Unaware, problem-aware | The buyer has pain but does not yet believe a product category exists or is worth changing for. | You need immediate payback, the problem is already obvious, or you lack patience for slow trust-building. | It creates category belief: 'this problem is real, solvable, and costly enough to act on.' | Teacher of the market. Your job is to make the invisible cost visible. | Expose the hidden cost, show the current workaround, define the new category, show the cost of staying still. | Research report, benchmark, before/after workflow, narrative essay, webinar, calculator, case study. | Returning visitors, newsletter signups, category searches, demo requests from educational journeys. | Awareness with no conversion path; vague thought leadership; audience that cannot buy. | Publish one 'state of the workflow' report using 10 interviews and turn it into ads, posts, and a webinar. | Talking about your product before the buyer accepts the problem. | Manufacturing fear or exaggerating risk to create false urgency. | Diffusion of Innovations; Jobs to Be Done; Binet & Field; Persuasion Knowledge Model |
| M04 | Comparison and alternatives pages | Demand capture and risk reduction | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer | Buyers compare you with known competitors, substitutes, manual workflows, or build-vs-buy choices. | You cannot be honest about tradeoffs or the competitor is not actually considered by your buyers. | Buyers already want choice architecture. Honest comparisons reduce uncertainty and prevent them from relying only on third-party narratives. | Trusted evaluator. Help the buyer choose correctly, even when that means disqualifying yourself. | Best for / not best for, tradeoffs, migration path, feature differences, pricing, security, integrations. | Comparison table, screenshots, customer quotes, migration guide, transparent limitations, pricing explanation. | Demo/trial conversion from comparison pages; assisted close rate; lower objection rate in sales calls. | Bounce from obvious bias, legal risk, competitor retaliation, wrong-fit signups. | Create one 'Product vs Alternative' page that includes a 'choose them if...' section. | Writing attack pages that destroy trust. | False claims about competitors; cherry-picking; disguised affiliate pages. | Persuasion Knowledge Model; Nielsen trust research; CMA/OECD dark patterns guidance |
| M05 | Retargeting / remarketing | Demand recapture | Problem-aware, solution-aware, vendor-aware | Visitors showed meaningful intent but did not convert: pricing page, docs, comparison, abandoned trial, webinar attendance. | You have no segmentation, weak consent/privacy posture, or the product is sensitive and retargeting feels invasive. | It reopens an incomplete decision loop. The best retargeting does not repeat the same claim; it answers the next objection. | Respectful reminder. You are not stalking; you are offering the next piece of evidence. | If they viewed pricing, show risk reversal. If they viewed docs, show integration proof. If they watched demo, show case study. | Segmented audiences, objection-specific ads, customer proof, demo clips, trial extensions, privacy-safe controls. | Incremental return visits and qualified conversions by segment. | Frequency fatigue, negative sentiment, privacy complaints, conversions that would have happened anyway. | Retarget only high-intent pages for 14 days with three proof messages, capped frequency, and exclude converters. | Showing the same generic ad to every visitor for months. | Creepy personalization; covert data use; targeting sensitive inferences. | Aguirre personalization paradox; FTC/OECD/CMA; Lewis & Rao measurement |
| M06 | Product demonstration ads | Proof and demand capture | Problem-aware, solution-aware, skeptical buyers | The product has a visible workflow, dramatic before/after, or a task that can be shown in seconds. | The demo requires too much context, the product is not visually understandable, or the shown workflow is not representative. | Demonstration asks for less belief. It lets the buyer inspect a concrete action instead of trusting abstract claims. | Show, do not declare. Let the product carry the claim. | Before state -> action -> result -> invite to test. | Short demo video, annotated screenshots, sample data, interactive demo, public changelog. | Demo-start rate, trial activation, quality of signups, watch-through to key proof moment. | Entertainment clicks without serious buyers; demo deception; over-polished fake workflows. | Create 3 short demos: 15-second pain/result, 45-second workflow, 2-minute technical walkthrough. | Showing UI screens without the buyer's job or outcome. | Using fake data/results or hiding setup complexity. | Fogg; TAM; Persuasion Knowledge Model; Rosengren et al. creativity |
| M07 | Interactive demo / sandbox | Risk reduction and activation | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, skeptical buyers | The buyer can understand value by trying a constrained version without full setup. | The product needs deep integration before value appears, or a fake sandbox would misrepresent the product. | The buyer converts borrowed belief into earned belief through direct inspection. | Give control. Let the buyer verify without pressure. | Try one realistic workflow with sample data; no credit card; see output before signup. | Sandbox environment, guided demo data, sample project, quick reset, transparent limitations. | Sandbox completion, aha-moment rate, trial-to-activated-user conversion. | Users playing without intent; confusion from unguided exploration; support burden. | Build a 3-step demo with one outcome and a CTA after value is visible. | Opening the full product too early with no guided path. | Making the sandbox easier or more capable than the real product. | TAM; Fogg; SaaS trial research; product-led growth evidence |
| M08 | Free trial | Product-led conversion | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer | Value can be experienced before payment and setup cost is reasonable. | The product requires human implementation, dirty data migration, or value only appears after long-term usage. | Trial reduces adoption risk and lets the product become evidence. | Trial is not free access; it is a designed proof sequence. | One clear success outcome, short setup, guided activation, proof of saved time/value, next plan recommendation. | Onboarding checklist, sample data, milestone emails, usage dashboard, support trigger, trial-end summary. | Activated trial-to-paid conversion, time to value, retained paid users. | Signups with no activation, support load, discount dependency, credit-card resentment. | Define the activation event and redesign the first 15 minutes to reach it faster. | Counting trial signups as success while activation is low. | Forced continuity, unclear cancellation, hidden trial end terms. | SaaS trial studies; Fogg; TAM; FTC dark patterns |
| M09 | Freemium | Product-led acquisition | Problem-aware through active buyer; especially low-friction categories | Marginal serving cost is low, users can self-onboard, and free usage creates habit, data, network effects, or upgrade triggers. | Free users are expensive, support-heavy, never hit premium pain, or the free tier satisfies the whole job. | Free removes price risk and creates usage-based evidence; premium converts when the free tier reveals a new constraint. | Design the free tier as value plus a natural ceiling, not a crippled product. | Start useful now; upgrade when you need scale, collaboration, automation, security, or control. | Clear plan limits, upgrade moments, team invites, usage dashboards, transparent pricing. | Free-to-paid conversion by cohort, product-qualified leads, retained revenue per acquired user. | Support cost, free-tier abuse, paid cannibalization, low intent signups. | Map free-user usage and identify the point where successful users naturally need a premium feature. | Making the free tier either useless or too complete. | Dark upgrade nudges, surprise lockouts, hostage data/export restrictions. | Zero-price effect; freemium research; Fogg; FTC/OECD |
| M10 | Product-led onboarding and activation | Conversion and retention | Trial user, free user, new paid user | Users sign up but fail to reach the aha moment or first valuable outcome. | The bigger problem is poor product-market fit, wrong traffic, or missing core value. | Most conversion loss happens between interest and experienced value. Onboarding reduces ability friction. | Guide to value; do not tour features. | Ask one setup question, route to one job, help complete one meaningful action, then expand. | Checklist, templates, sample data, contextual prompts, setup wizard, activation analytics. | Activation rate, time to first value, retention after activation. | Checklist completion without value, notification fatigue, premature upsell. | Remove all non-essential steps before the first valuable output. | Teaching the UI instead of helping the buyer finish their job. | Manipulative defaults, consent preselection, friction to leave. | Fogg; TAM; SaaS trial/freemium research; CMA choice architecture |
| M11 | Lifecycle email and in-app nurture | Conversion, retention, expansion | Lead, trial user, free user, paid user | The buyer needs staged evidence, reminders, use-case education, or activation guidance over time. | You have nothing useful to say, segmentation is weak, or frequency will create annoyance. | Complex buying rarely resolves in one session. Lifecycle messages sequence proof and reduce next-step ambiguity. | Sequence the buyer's next useful action, not your next demand. | Trigger-based: based on behavior, role, use case, missing setup, or objection. | Email flows, in-app messages, behavior triggers, case studies, templates, product tips. | Activated conversions, retained cohorts, expansion actions, reply/engagement quality. | Unsubscribes, spam complaints, low-quality clicks, user frustration. | Build a 5-message activation sequence tied to actual product milestones. | Sending a generic newsletter to every stage. | Fake urgency, misleading subject lines, hard-to-unsubscribe flows. | Fogg; Consumer Decision Journey; FTC dark patterns |
| M12 | Case studies | Trust and risk reduction | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer | Buyers need evidence that similar people achieved a credible outcome. | You only have vanity quotes, cannot quantify the before/after, or the customer is not representative. | Case studies reduce perceived implementation and outcome risk through similarity and narrative proof. | Evidence file, not celebration piece. | Context -> problem -> alternatives considered -> implementation -> quantified outcome -> limitations. | Customer interview, metrics, screenshots, stakeholder quote, implementation timeline. | Assisted pipeline/close rate, sales-cycle reduction, objection reduction. | Overclaiming ROI, cherry-picked outliers, mismatched personas. | Write one rigorous case study with numbers and a 'what made this work' section. | Publishing vague 'customer loves us' stories. | Invented or inflated results; hiding selection bias. | Nielsen trust; source credibility meta-analysis; B2B buyer reports |
| M13 | Reviews and testimonials | Trust and independent proof | Solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer | Buyers seek peer evidence or the category has established review channels. | The product experience is weak, reviews are incentivized without transparency, or support cannot handle criticism. | Buyers distrust brand claims but inspect other buyers' experiences, especially when reviews are specific and balanced. | Earned reputation. Make real experience visible. | Surface specific use cases, tradeoffs, role/company context, and recurring themes. | Review site profile, testimonial library, quote permissions, review request workflow, response process. | Review volume/quality, conversion lift on pages using reviews, win rate where reviews are cited. | Review manipulation, low-star pattern, testimonial mismatch. | Ask 20 successful users for specific reviews using prompts about use case and outcome. | Using generic five-star quotes that say nothing. | Fake reviews, undisclosed incentives, suppressing negative feedback. | Online review meta-analysis; Nielsen trust; FTC endorsement guidance/dark patterns |
| M14 | Referral / word-of-mouth systems | Trust and acquisition | Problem-aware through active buyer | Users are satisfied, the product is shareable, and a recommendation reduces adoption risk. | Retention is weak, the product is embarrassing/private, or incentives distort quality. | People trust peers more than ads. Referrals borrow trust from existing relationships. | Make successful users generous and specific. | Help users introduce the product as a solution to a concrete peer problem. | Referral prompt, shareable output, invite link, team collaboration, rewards, case-specific templates. | Referral conversion, referred-user retention, viral coefficient, expansion from team invites. | Spammy invites, reward gaming, low-quality signups. | Add referral prompt after a successful outcome, not at signup. | Asking users to share before they have value. | Dark social spam, undisclosed incentives, aggressive contact importing. | Nielsen trust; diffusion theory; Fogg |
| M15 | Community-led growth | Trust, education, retention | Problem-aware, solution-aware, user/adopter | The problem space benefits from shared learning, identity, practice, templates, or peer support. | You want a cheap acquisition hack, cannot moderate, or the product does not support ongoing discussion. | Communities build familiarity, peer proof, and category belief over time. | Host the problem space, not a billboard for your product. | Useful discussions, expert sessions, templates, critique, support, and peer examples. | Slack/Discord/forum, events, moderation rules, content archive, community case studies. | Active members, contribution quality, product-qualified community leads, retention. | Low-quality engagement, support burden, community becoming sales-only. | Run a 4-week cohort or office-hours series around one painful workflow. | Launching a community before there is a real shared need. | Astroturfing; hiding commercial intent; manipulating discussions. | Diffusion of Innovations; trust research; Persuasion Knowledge Model |
| M16 | Founder-led / expert-led content | Authority and trust | Unaware through vendor-aware | The buyer values technical credibility, category insight, strong opinions, or behind-the-scenes competence. | The founder lacks useful insight, content becomes ego performance, or claims are not backed by product reality. | Visible expertise can reduce trust gaps, especially for technical or emerging categories. | Operator explaining the truth of the market. | Teach hard-won insight, show tradeoffs, demonstrate work, reveal product reasoning. | Essays, teardown threads, technical videos, open roadmap/changelog, benchmarks, demo builds. | High-quality inbound, newsletter quality, demo requests referencing content, community pull. | Audience without buyer fit, personality dependence, controversy without conversion. | Publish 6 technical/product essays answering objections heard in sales or support. | Posting generic motivation instead of useful market insight. | Cult-of-personality manipulation; overstating expertise. | Source credibility meta-analysis; Persuasion Knowledge Model; Jobs to Be Done |
| M17 | Webinars / workshops / live demos | Education and sales enablement | Problem-aware, solution-aware, vendor-aware | The buyer needs education, stakeholder alignment, or a guided view of a complex workflow. | The product is simple enough for self-serve, or you will deliver a disguised sales pitch with little value. | Live format creates attention, interaction, and contextual proof that static pages cannot. | Help them make the decision smarter. | Teach the workflow, show the solution, answer objections, offer a next diagnostic/demo. | Registration page, deck, live demo, Q&A, recording, follow-up sequence, workbook. | Attendance quality, meeting requests, pipeline from attendees, activation after event. | Registrations without attendance, low-fit audience, content fatigue. | Host one practical workshop around a painful job, with product demo only after teaching the decision criteria. | Treating webinars as long ads. | Bait-and-switch education into pressure selling. | Fogg; Consumer Decision Journey; B2B buyer reports |
| M18 | Sales-assisted demo and discovery | Complex conversion | Vendor-aware, active buyer | Price, risk, implementation, or stakeholder complexity is high enough that self-serve proof is insufficient. | Buyers clearly prefer self-serve and the salesperson adds friction instead of clarity. | A good sales process turns general proof into buyer-specific confidence. | Consultant and risk reducer. Diagnose before demonstrating. | Understand current state, quantify pain, map stakeholders, show relevant workflow, define next step. | Discovery script, tailored demo, security docs, mutual action plan, ROI model, implementation guide. | Qualified win rate, sales-cycle length, proof-of-concept conversion, expansion potential. | Bad-fit closes, buyer fatigue, overcustomized demos, discounting. | Redesign demo flow to start with buyer job and objection map, not feature tour. | Using one generic demo for every buyer. | Pressure tactics, false scarcity, hiding implementation cost. | B2B buyer reports; TAM; Jobs to Be Done; FTC/OECD/CMA |
| M19 | Account-based marketing (ABM) | Enterprise demand creation/capture | Target accounts across problem-aware to active buyer | A small set of high-value accounts can justify tailored research, messaging, and multi-stakeholder orchestration. | Deal sizes are too small, account data is poor, or you cannot coordinate marketing/sales/customer success. | Enterprise buying is account-level, not just individual-level. ABM aligns proof to stakeholders and account context. | Precision campaign architect. One account is a market. | Account-specific pain, industry proof, stakeholder-specific value, tailored next step. | Target account list, persona map, account research, tailored landing pages, case studies, sales sequences. | Target account engagement, opportunity creation, pipeline, win rate, deal expansion. | Personalization theatre, creepiness, wasted effort on wrong accounts. | Pick 20 high-fit accounts and build industry-specific proof + outbound + retargeting + sales follow-up. | Calling generic ads 'ABM' because the audience is narrow. | Creepy personalization or misuse of personal data. | B2B buyer reports; personalization paradox; source credibility |
| M20 | Outbound email / LinkedIn | Demand creation and targeted acquisition | Problem-aware or likely-problem accounts | You can identify a high-fit buyer and write a relevant, respectful message tied to a real business pain. | You are guessing, blasting lists, making vague claims, or relying on volume to compensate for irrelevance. | Good outbound interrupts only because it is unusually relevant and lowers effort for a buyer who has a real problem. | Earn the interruption. | Specific observation -> likely problem -> small proof -> low-friction offer -> easy opt-out. | ICP list, trigger events, concise copy, relevant case study, calendar/demo path, opt-out handling. | Positive reply rate, qualified meetings, opportunity quality. | Spam complaints, domain reputation, low-fit meetings, brand damage. | Send 30 highly researched messages to one ICP with one concrete pain and one proof asset. | Personalized first line followed by generic pitch. | Spam, deceptive subject lines, scraping misuse, no opt-out. | Persuasion Knowledge Model; source credibility; privacy/dark pattern guidance |
| M21 | Partner / integration co-marketing | Trust and distribution | Solution-aware, vendor-aware | Your product depends on or complements another trusted platform, integration, agency, consultant, or ecosystem. | The integration is weak, the partner's audience is misaligned, or the partnership is only logo-swapping. | Borrowed context and trusted distribution reduce discovery and trust friction. | Ecosystem builder. Make both audiences more successful. | Show the combined workflow and explain why the integration removes a specific bottleneck. | Integration page, joint demo, app marketplace listing, partner webinar, implementation guide. | Partner-sourced pipeline, integration activation, co-marketing conversion, retention uplift. | Low-quality partner leads, dependency risk, confusing ownership. | Create one joint workflow demo with a complementary product and promote to both audiences. | Partnership announced without a usable joint workflow. | Implying endorsement or integration depth that does not exist. | Trust/source credibility; diffusion; B2B buyer reports |
| M22 | Marketplace / app store listing optimization | Demand capture | Solution-aware, active buyer | Buyers search in ecosystems such as Chrome Web Store, Slack/Atlassian/Salesforce/Shopify/HubSpot marketplaces, mobile app stores, or cloud marketplaces. | The category is not marketplace-driven or reviews/support are not ready. | Marketplace search carries high intent and borrowed trust from the platform ecosystem. | Shelf discipline. Make the product instantly inspectable in the buyer's chosen store. | Clear category, screenshots, use cases, reviews, permissions, setup time, support path. | Listing copy, screenshots, demo video, reviews, category tags, integration docs, support SLA. | Listing views to install, install-to-activation, review rating, marketplace-sourced revenue. | Installs without activation, bad reviews, platform dependency. | Rewrite listing around one primary use case and add a 60-second workflow demo. | Using generic homepage copy inside the marketplace. | Misleading permissions, fake reviews, hiding data access. | TAM; source credibility; online reviews |
| M23 | Pricing and packaging strategy | Conversion and monetization | Vendor-aware, active buyer, expansion | People understand the product but hesitate on value, plan fit, risk, or procurement. | You are changing price to compensate for unclear value or poor retention. | Pricing communicates who the product is for, what value scales with, and how much risk the buyer carries. | Price is architecture, not a number. | Plans should map to buyer maturity, usage, collaboration, control, security, or scale. | Pricing page, plan comparison, calculator, FAQ, plan recommendation logic, upgrade paths. | Paid conversion, ARPA, expansion, churn, support/sales friction by plan. | Confusion, price objections, downgrade gaming, hidden-fee backlash. | Interview lost deals/trial users about pricing confusion, then simplify plan names and upgrade triggers. | Copying competitor pricing without understanding value metric. | Drip pricing, hidden fees, forced bundles, cancellation sludge. | Freemium/zero-price; drip pricing research; FTC/OECD/CMA |
| M24 | Risk reversal and trust infrastructure | Risk reduction | Vendor-aware, active buyer | Buyers worry about wasting money, implementation failure, security, cancellation, data lock-in, or internal embarrassment. | You cannot operationally honor the promise or the product has unresolved reliability issues. | Many buyers are not unconvinced about value; they are afraid of being wrong. | Remove fear honestly. | No credit card, cancel anytime, export data, security docs, SLA, onboarding support, refund/guarantee where appropriate. | Security page, trust center, cancellation policy, refund policy, data export docs, implementation plan. | Conversion lift at risk-heavy steps, reduced sales objections, trial-to-paid conversion. | Guarantee abuse, legal exposure, promises support cannot fulfill. | Add a visible 'What happens if this is not for me?' section to pricing and checkout. | Ignoring the buyer's fear because the team is obsessed with features. | Making promises hard to exercise; hiding cancellation/export paths. | TAM; B2B buyer reports; FTC/OECD/CMA |
| M25 | Landing page conversion optimization (CRO) | Conversion | All, but especially paid traffic and active buyers | Qualified traffic arrives but does not inspect, sign up, request demo, or continue. | Traffic quality is poor, sample sizes are too small, or teams want button-color changes instead of value clarity. | CRO reduces friction, ambiguity, and mismatch between buyer intent and proof path. | Decision architect. Every section must answer a buyer question. | Who it is for -> problem -> outcome -> proof -> how it works -> objections -> next step. | Landing page variants, analytics, session recordings, user tests, heatmaps, survey responses. | Qualified conversion rate, activation after conversion, revenue per visitor. | Conversion lift with worse customer quality; false positives from underpowered tests. | Rewrite the page for one buyer state and one job, then test against the generic page. | Optimizing microcopy before fixing the offer and proof. | Dark patterns, forced action, hidden terms. | Fogg; TAM; Lewis/Rao measurement; CMA choice architecture |
| M26 | A/B testing and incrementality testing | Measurement | All | You need to know whether a change caused improvement rather than merely correlated with it. | Traffic is too small, the decision is low-risk, or you cannot define a meaningful metric. | Experiments reduce self-deception. They reveal whether your method created incremental behavior. | Scientist, not believer. | Hypothesis -> variant -> randomization/holdout -> primary metric -> guardrails -> decision rule. | Experiment plan, analytics instrumentation, holdout group, power estimate, post-test analysis. | Incremental lift in meaningful outcome: activated trial, paid conversion, retained revenue. | Sample ratio mismatch, novelty effects, short-term lift with long-term harm. | Run one landing-page or email test with a prewritten decision rule and a guardrail metric. | Calling before/after comparisons 'experiments'. | Experimenting on users without regard to harm or consent where needed. | Lewis/Rao/Reiley; Gordon et al.; Johnson/Lewis/Reiley; eBay paid-search experiment |
| M27 | Lead magnets and newsletters | Education and nurture | Unaware, problem-aware, solution-aware | The buying cycle is long and you can repeatedly provide useful material before purchase. | You use low-value gated PDFs to collect emails from people with no buying intent. | It creates a permissioned channel for staged trust-building and problem education. | Earn permission with substance. | Useful framework, benchmark, calculator, template, teardown, or diagnostic that makes the buyer smarter. | Newsletter, report, template, email sequence, segmentation, landing page. | Subscriber-to-qualified-lead rate, replies, content-assisted conversions. | List growth with low intent, unsubscribe rate, spam complaints. | Create one ungated strong article and offer a related template/calculator as optional follow-up. | Mistaking email capture for buyer progress. | Bait-and-switch content, misleading forms, aggressive nurture. | Consumer Decision Journey; Fogg; Persuasion Knowledge Model |
| M28 | Influencer / creator / analyst relations | Trust and reach | Unaware through vendor-aware | Your buyers follow trusted experts, creators, consultants, analysts, or community leaders. | Influencer fit is weak, disclosure is unclear, or the audience is broad but not qualified. | Credible third parties can create attention and borrowed trust, especially when they demonstrate the product honestly. | Borrow trust carefully; never rent fake belief. | Expert uses or evaluates the product for a real job, including tradeoffs. | Brief, demo account, disclosure language, content rights, honest review terms, tracking. | Qualified traffic, attributed trials, brand search lift, review quality, assisted conversions. | Vanity reach, audience mismatch, trust backlash. | Partner with 3 niche experts for honest workflow demos, not generic endorsements. | Buying posts from creators whose audience will never use the product. | Undisclosed sponsorship, scripted fake reviews, inflated claims. | Source credibility meta-analysis; online reviews; FTC endorsement/dark pattern guidance |
| M29 | Launch campaigns / waitlists / beta programs | Demand creation and validation | Unaware, problem-aware, early adopters | You need market feedback, early adopters, buzz, or a concentrated proof event. | The product is not ready for scrutiny or the launch will attract the wrong audience. | Launches create a temporary attention window and social signal; betas recruit tolerant early users. | Use attention to learn, not just to announce. | Problem, who it is for, why now, what is ready, what is still limited, how to join/test. | Launch page, demo, waitlist, beta onboarding, feedback loop, public roadmap. | Qualified waitlist, activation from launch, feedback quality, retention of beta users. | Hype without retention, wrong-fit users, support overload. | Run a small beta with explicit use-case qualification and structured feedback calls. | Optimizing for upvotes/signups instead of activated learning. | False scarcity, fake social proof, hiding beta limitations. | Diffusion of Innovations; Fogg; dark patterns guidance |
| M30 | Reactivation / winback | Retention and revenue recovery | Dormant lead, inactive trial/user, churned customer | People had real intent or value before but stopped due to timing, friction, missing feature, price, or competing priorities. | The user was wrong-fit, churned angrily, or the reason for leaving has not changed. | Prior familiarity lowers discovery cost. A relevant change can reopen the decision. | Respect the previous no. Return only with a changed reason. | You tried X; since then Y changed; here is why it may now be worth another look. | Churn reason tags, product updates, personalized examples, special migration/support, clear opt-out. | Reactivated accounts, retained reactivation revenue, reply quality. | Spam complaints, discount-only return, churn repeats. | Segment churned users by reason and send only the segment whose objection is now solved. | Generic 'we miss you' emails with no substantive change. | Guilt framing, misleading 'account will close' messages, hard opt-out. | Fogg; lifecycle marketing; FTC/OECD/CMA |
| M31 | Expansion / upsell / cross-sell | Revenue expansion | Successful paid user/team/account | The customer has achieved value and now faces scale, collaboration, security, automation, or governance needs. | The customer has not adopted the core product or upsell would hide the real problem. | Existing trust and usage data reveal the next job better than cold acquisition can. | Expand only after value is proven. | You are already doing X; unlock Y to remove the next bottleneck. | Usage signals, health scores, plan limits, team invitations, admin/security features, success review. | Net revenue retention, expansion rate, seat/team growth, retained expansion. | Upsell frustration, feature hostage-taking, churn from overcharging. | Identify one usage threshold that predicts successful upgrade and trigger a helpful in-product prompt. | Upselling before the customer sees core value. | Artificial limits, data hostage tactics, surprise plan changes. | Product-led growth/SaaS trial research; Fogg; dark patterns guidance |
| M32 | Customer success as marketing | Retention, expansion, proof creation | Paid customer and future buyer references | Customers need help reaching outcomes and their success can become proof for similar buyers. | You are trying to patch product weakness with manual support forever. | Successful customers create retention, expansion, referrals, reviews, and case studies. | Make the promise true. | Outcome plan, milestone review, usage recommendations, problem prevention, story capture. | Success plan, health dashboard, quarterly review, case-study process, referral triggers. | Retention, expansion, time-to-value, referenceability, case-study creation. | High-touch cost, dependency on manual heroics, support debt. | For 10 new customers, create a 30-day outcome plan and ask for proof only after outcome achieved. | Treating customer success as reactive support. | Overpromising outcomes in sales and forcing success teams to compensate. | B2B buying; trust research; Jobs to Be Done |
